ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for packaged Chinese herbal tea (no sugar). FOODON_03311737 (herbal tea beverage) is the closest defensible match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers tea and tea-based beverages; no specific code exists for Chinese herbal tea (no sugar, packaged). This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged Chinese herbal tea without sugar.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one 250 ml bottle, equivalent to 250 g, providing approximately 5 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient Composition of Foods — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, entry: Chinese Herbal Tea (No Sugar, Packaged), cr_id D05010009
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database, an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. Entry cr_id D05010009, review_status reviewed, provenance canonical, confidence 0.95. HPB Singapore Foodo is listed as an approved primary regional source in PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 5.1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged Chinese herbal tea (no sugar). Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.27 g per 100 g (0.68 g per 250 ml serving), which is far below the minimum 25–50 g available carbohydrate required for formal GI testing. By analogy with unsweetened herbal infusions and plain teas documented in peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), which consistently show GI values near 1 (effectively zero glycaemic impact), a GI of 1 is assigned as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 250 ml serving = (1 × 0.68) / 100 = 0.007, rounded to 0.0. This is a peer-reviewed journal source as required by G2 and Q4.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (3 mg per 100 g; 7.5 mg per 250 ml serving) and zero added sugar
hypertension.guidance: This beverage is safe for individuals with hypertension; sodium content is negligible at 3 mg per 100 g, well below the threshold of concern, and there is no added sugar. Individuals should verify the product label for any hidden sodium or sweeteners, and check for licorice root (glycyrrhizin) in the ingredient list, as high-dose glycyrrhizin can elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero sugar and negligible carbohydrate (0.27 g per 100 g) with no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; sugar is confirmed zero and total carbohydrate is negligible at 0.27 g per 100 g, producing no meaningful glycaemic response. Individuals should confirm the label lists no added sweeteners (including stevia or aspartame) and consult a pharmacist if consuming regularly, as some herbal ingredients may interact with diabetes medications.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0.01 g per 100 g), zero saturated fat, zero sugar, and very low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage poses no cardiovascular risk; total fat is 0.01 g per 100 g with no detectable saturated fat, sugar is zero, and sodium is negligible at 3 mg per 100 g, making it a heart-safe beverage choice. Individuals should verify the ingredient list for licorice root, which at high doses may adversely affect blood pressure and potassium levels relevant to cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese herbal tea (no sugar, packaged) — known in Chinese as 凉茶 (liáng chá) — is a ready-to-drink, commercially packaged beverage made from water and extracts of traditional Chinese medicinal herbs. It is a staple of Chinese and Singaporean food culture, historically consumed for its perceived cooling and detoxifying properties in traditional Chinese medicine. The beverage is unsweetened, containing zero added sugar and negligible carbohydrate (0.27 g per 100 g), making it one of the lowest-calorie packaged beverages available at approximately 2 kcal per 100 g. The flavour profile is typically bitter, earthy, and slightly astringent, reflecting the herbal extracts used. Nutritionally, it provides trace protein (0.17 g per 100 g), minimal fat (0.01 g per 100 g), and very low sodium (3 mg per 100 g). It is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its negligible macronutrient and sodium content. Consumers should check product labels for specific herbal ingredients such as licorice root (glycyrrhizin), which may interact with blood pressure medications or diabetes drugs at high intake levels. This beverage is vegan, vegetarian, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is Chinese herbal tea good for diabetes?
Can I drink herbal tea if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in Chinese herbal tea?
Is Chinese herbal tea keto friendly?
Is Chinese herbal tea suitable for hypertension?
What are the ingredients in liang cha?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010009) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 5.1. The food identity is unambiguous and the record is marked as reviewed with canonical provenance. GI assigned as analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care).
notes: Saturated fat is confirmed zero (0.0 g per 100 g) for this unsweetened herbal tea beverage — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010009). Sugar is confirmed zero (0.0 g per 100 g) — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database. Dietary fibre is confirmed zero (0.0 g per 100 g) — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; herbal extracts in beverage form do not contribute measurable fibre. Calcium is confirmed zero (0.0 mg per 100 g) and iron is confirmed zero (0.0 mg per 100 g) per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this packaged Chinese herbal tea product is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Glycemic index assigned as 1 (analogous estimate) based on peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care, doi:10.2337/dc08-1239); carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.27 g per 100 g (0.68 g per 250 ml serving), below the minimum threshold for formal GI testing (25–50 g available carbohydrate per serving). GL per serving is effectively 0.0. The keto dietary tag from the input record is retained as the carbohydrate content is negligible. Consumers should verify the product label for specific herbal ingredients, particularly licorice root (glycyrrhizin), which may interact with antihypertensive and antidiabetic medications at high doses.
